metadata.title: Naked Statistics: Stripping the Dread from the Data — Book Review

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Descriptive statistics collapse messy complexity into a single comprehensible number or ranking. Whoever sets the weighting controls the ranking, so changing the weights changes who wins. Mean salary obscures inequality because a few high earners drag the average upward. The median reveals the typical case by ignoring those extreme outliers entirely.

artifactLines: Descriptive statistics collapse messy complexity into a single comprehensible number or ranking.
Whoever sets the weighting controls the ranking, so changing the weights changes who wins.
Mean salary obscures inequality because a few high earners drag the average upward.
The median reveals the typical case by ignoring those extreme outliers entirely.

beats.narration_text: I want to actually feel how reweighting flips a ranking, the way changing the weighting changed the answer here. Take a small set of, say, five universities with made-up scores for tuition, graduation rate, and salary. Show me: (1) a ranking where salary dominates the weighting, (2) the same schools re-ranked when graduation rate dominates instead, and (3) a worked mean-versus-median salary example proving how the mean hides the inequality the median exposes.
